notable.notable_blurb: Painter of Connecticut's fabled Charter Oak
notable.primary_url_label: Rhode Island Heritage Hall of Fame

narrative_blocks.header: He painted the Charter Oak after it fell
narrative_blocks.body: Brownell lived opposite Hartford's Charter Oak and painted it in 1857, the year after the historic tree came down in a storm. His canvas became the tree's definitive likeness and hangs today at the Wadsworth Atheneum.

narrative_blocks.header: He left the law for landscape painting
narrative_blocks.body: Admitted to the Connecticut bar in 1843, Brownell abandoned law after a decade when illness and a love of sketching drew him to art. He studied in Hartford and kept a studio there before moving to New York in 1860.

narrative_blocks.header: He became America's painter of Cuba
narrative_blocks.body: Brownell spent seven winters on the Cuban sugar plantations of his mother's family, the De Wolfs of Bristol, becoming perhaps the mid-century American artist most associated with the island. He befriended the Hudson River School painter Frederic Church, a fellow son of Hartford.

narrative_blocks.header: He married into the Metcalf family's orbit
narrative_blocks.body: In 1865 Brownell married Henrietta Knowlton Angell Pierce of Bristol and became stepfather to her daughter Esther, the future wife of Providence Journal publisher Stephen Olney Metcalf. He settled in Bristol, where the couple raised four sons.

narrative_blocks.header: His diary was written in Greek letters
narrative_blocks.body: For years the restless Brownell kept a curious diary, English words spelled out in the Greek alphabet. He traveled almost ceaselessly, from the White Mountains and Cuba to Egypt and South America, until a broken hip slowed him near the end.

cross_connections.display_label: founded the Atheneum that holds this Charter Oak
by tradition hid the charter in the oak he painted

INST052.name: Wadsworth Atheneum Museum of Art
INST052.notes: Founded 1842 by Daniel Wadsworth (HD1330); opened 1844. Oldest continuously operating public art museum in the United States. The building was designed to house Daniel's personal art collection and establish Hartford as a cultural center. Daniel was a patron of the Hudson River School — Thomas Cole and Frederic Church both exhibited here. The museum holds ART030: John Trumbull's 1784 portrait of Daniel (age 13) and his father Jeremiah (T00387) — painted 58 years before the museum existed, now hanging in the institution Daniel built. A portrait becomes its own home. Daniel Wadsworth descends from BOTH Thomas Hooker (through his mother H00362 Mehitable Russell → H00118 Mary Pierpont → H00034 Mary Hooker Pierpont) AND from John Talcott (through his father T00387 Jeremiah → T00068 Abigail Talcott → T00045 Gov. Joseph Talcott → T00035 Lt.-Col. John Talcott Jr.). Two founding Hartford lines united in one institution.
